JavaScript Core Concepts (47): Why Does JavaScript Even Have Prototypes?
In this post we're going to dig into what a prototype actually is in JavaScript.

Before we get into this, we need a bit of history about where JavaScript came from. Back in the early days, JavaScript actually had a loose connection to Java. The names look really similar, and some people even assumed JavaScript

Java — you'll sometimes see it written as "Java Script" to try and tell them apart. But honestly, the name was a marketing move. When JavaScript first launched, it was deliberately named that way to ride the popularity of Java, which was the hot language at the time.
That history is also why JavaScript has a
keyword for creating an object. For Java developers that felt familiar and welcoming. But under the hood, these two languages work completely differently.
Java uses class-based inheritance, while JavaScript uses prototypal inheritance — and in practice those two approaches are very different.

So what is object-oriented programming? Here's an example. Say we want to define a dog. We all know "dog" is just a broad label — there are tons of breeds out there, like poodles, Taiwan dogs, Pomeranians, and so on. And that's before you even get to the fact that every dog has a different coat color.

So for "dog" we can define a class. Inside it we put the behaviors every dog is guaranteed to have — like barking — while things like body size and coat color are specific to each dog. Then, whenever we want to create different dogs, we just call this class, and every dog inherits the barking behavior.
A good way to think about it: the dog class is just a concept (a class) — it has no life of its own. When we want to turn it into an actual, living dog, we use
(an instance) to bring it to life.
So why does JavaScript use prototypal inheritance? The main reason is that JavaScript itself is built on objects — everything in it is created as an object — so it doesn't have the concept of a
Because of that, we have to use the idea of prototypes to mimic something class-like. Now, I just said JavaScript doesn't have classes, and at this point someone might ask: what about the ES6
? The thing is, that's just syntactic sugar. Under the hood it's still prototypal inheritance doing the work.

Time to dig into what a prototype actually is in JavaScript. We'll start with a bit of history — why JavaScript got named the way it did, how it relates to Java, and why that history is the reason JavaScript uses prototypal inheritance instead of class-based inheritance. By the end you'll understand where prototypes come from and why the language needs them.
